trajectory.description: Observation: Evaluation failed due to PyTorch 2.6 changing the default of `weights_only` to True in `torch.load`, which prevents loading the checkpoint containing numpy arrays (mean, std, adj_norm). Analysis: We need to explicitly set `weights_only=False` in `torch.load` inside `predict.py`. Decision: Update `predict.py` and resubmit evaluation to get the baseline score.

trajectory.description: Observation: The baseline STGCN model achieved a mae_60min of 5.3372. Analysis: The baseline model is very simple and predicts all 12 steps from the last hidden state. To significantly improve performance, we can implement a Graph WaveNet (GWNet) architecture, which is highly effective for spatiotemporal traffic forecasting. GWNet uses dilated causal convolutions for temporal dependencies and diffusion graph convolutions for spatial dependencies, along with an adaptive adjacency matrix. Decision: Implement GWNet in `model.py`, update `train.py` and `predict.py` to use it, and train for 20 epochs to evaluate its performance.

trajectory.description: Observation: The baseline GWNet achieved mae_60min of 3.9954. Analysis: The model can be further improved by using the full GWNet architecture (8 layers with dilations 1,2,4,8,1,2,4,8), adding forward and backward random walk transition matrices, and training for more epochs (40) with a MultiStepLR scheduler. I will also add AMP (Automatic Mixed Precision) to speed up training. Decision: Update model.py, train.py, and predict.py with these improvements, train for 40 epochs, and submit for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The GWNet model achieved mae_60min of 3.6686. The training curve shows the validation loss is still decreasing, and the model capacity can be increased. Analysis: We can improve the model by increasing the number of channels (residual_channels and dilation_channels from 32 to 64), increasing the node embedding dimension (from 10 to 40), and training for more epochs (60). We will also switch to AdamW optimizer and OneCycleLR scheduler for better convergence. Decision: Update model.py, train.py, and predict.py with these changes, train for 60 epochs, and submit for evaluation.

[eval_harness] Phase 2: Running evaluator
[eval_harness] Result: {'mae_avg': 2.9153, 'rmse': 6.2472, 'mape': 8.1059, 'mae_15min': 2.516, 'mae_30min': 2.9782, 'mae_60min': 3.4475}

trajectory.description: Observation: The training failed with a shape mismatch error: 'The size of tensor a (12) must match the size of tensor b (207) at non-singleton dimension 2'. Analysis: This indicates that the model output `out` and the target `y` had incompatible shapes during the loss calculation. `y` has shape `[batch_size, 12, 207]`. The error message implies `out` had shape `[batch_size, 207, 12]` or `[batch_size, 12, 207, 1]`, which caused `broadcast_tensors` to fail. This was likely due to a missing or incorrect `squeeze(-1)` at the end of the `forward` method in `model.py` when I rewrote it in the previous iteration. Decision: I will rewrite `model.py` carefully, ensuring the output shape is explicitly squeezed to `[batch_size, 12, 207]`. I will also add a shape assertion in `train.py` to catch any future mismatches early. I will then resume the training for 60 epochs with the increased capacity model.

trajectory.description: Observation: The GWNet model achieved mae_60min of 3.4475. The training took ~32 minutes for 60 epochs. We have 1.84 hours left. Analysis: We can further improve the model by: 1) Increasing model capacity (channels from 64 to 96, node_dim from 40 to 64). 2) Using a Weighted MAE loss that gives more weight to later time steps, especially the 12th step, since the primary metric is mae_60min. 3) Changing the model selection criterion during validation to use mae_60min instead of the average MAE across all steps. 4) Training for 100 epochs, which should take ~70 minutes and fit well within the remaining time budget. Decision: Update model.py, train.py, and predict.py with these improvements, train for 100 epochs, and submit for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The model from iter 7 achieved mae_60min of 3.5058, which is worse than the iter 6 model (3.4475). The increased capacity and Weighted MAE loss likely caused overfitting or instability. We have 43 minutes left in the time budget. Analysis: To get the best possible score before time runs out, we should revert to the iter 6 architecture (channels=64, node_dim=40) and standard L1 loss, which proved to be highly effective. We will train for 70 epochs, which will take approximately 38 minutes, fitting safely within our remaining 43-minute budget. We will also revert to using val_mae_avg for model selection, as it provided a more stable signal in iter 6. Decision: Update model.py, train.py, and predict.py to revert to the iter 6 configuration, train for 70 epochs, and submit the final evaluation.
